english: prob. the original form of bsnár-ba (bsnar ba).
n. of one of the lunar mansions, v. rgyu-skar (rgyu skar) 3.
thread, silk-thread, woolen thread etc.; knitting-yarn, or yarn used for other purposes; also for warp, abb-yarn.

pf. and fut. bsnun (bsnun), 1. to prick Lt.; to stick or prick into, e.g. a stick into the ground Mil., mtson (mtshon) a weapon Lex. - 2. to suckle (cf. nú-ba (nu ba), núd-pa (nud pa)), nú-ma (nu ma) or nú-żo snún-pa (nu zho snun pa) Pth., Lt., id. - 3. to multiply Wdk. - gnad snún-pa (gnad snun pa) Lex. w.e., Sch.: 'to excavate the interior, to get or penetrate into the inside'(?).

pf. bsnubs (bsnubs), fut. bsnub (bsnub), imp. snub(s) (snubs) vb. a. to núb-pa (nub pa), to cause to perish; gen. fig. to suppress, abolish, abrogate, annul, destroy, annihilate, a religion, a custom etc.

(S.g., -po (-po) Cs.), 1. fat, grease, any greasy substance, snúm-gyis skúd-pa (snum gyis skud pa) to grease, to smear; in C. esp. oil (W. *már-nag* (*mar nag*)), snum-zád-kyi már-me (snum zad kyi mar me) a lamp, the oil of which is consumed; also fig., snum (snum) being added pleon., e. g. Miṅ.: lus-zúṅs snum-zád (lus zungs snum zad), and parallel to it: lus-zúṅs zad (lus zungs zad) Lt.; rlan-snúm (rlan snum) raw fat, żun-snúm (zhun snum) melted fat Cs.; sol-snúm (sol snum) cart-grease, composed of pulverized charcoal and fat Glr. - 2. fig. of luxuriant grass or pasture, ri snúm-pa (ri snum pa) a hill clothed with luxuriant pastures C. (cf. rug-gé (rug ge)); snúm-la jám-pa (snum la 'jam pa) luxurious and soft Mil. - snum-°kóṅ (snum 'kong) a little bowl for oil etc. - snum-°kúr (snum 'kur) a kind of pastry baked in suet. - snum-glégs (snum glegs), W. *num-lág* (*num lag*), a wooden tablet, blackened, greased, and strewed with ashes, used for writing upon with a wood-pencil, thus serving for a slate. - snúm-can (snum can), snúm-bcas (snum bcas), snum-ldán (snum ldan) fat, oily, greasy. - snúm-dri (snum dri) a smell of fat. - snúm-nag (snum nag) oil Kun. - snúm-rtsi (snum rtsi) a greasy liquid, oil etc.; greasy, oily C. snúm-pa (snum pa) vb. = snóm-pa (snom pa) I.

pf. and fut. bsnur (bsnur), vb. a. to núr-ba (nur ba), 1. to put or move out of its place, to remove, to shift W.: to move or draw towards one's self Cs., so mdún-du snúr-ba (mdun du snur ba) Zam. is explained by °tén-pa ('then pa). - 2. Sch.: to cut into pieces, to fracture, to crush, żíb-mor (zhib mor) into small pieces (to reduce), to powder; so it seems to be frq. used in Lt., though one Lex. explains it by °dás-pa ('das pa) (scarcely corr.). - 3. Cs. to bring near = to shorten, dus (dus) a term, a space of time. Cf. brnú(r)-ba (brnu(r) ba) Lexx.

1. extremity, end, snál-mai sne (snal mai sne) Lex., of a thread, tág-sne (thag sne) the end of a rope Sch.; hem, seam, né-mo (*ne mo*) *ltáb-ce* (*ltab ce*) W. to fold down and sew the edge of a piece of cloth, to hem; *né-mo gyáb-ce* (*ne mo rgyab ce*) W. to trim with cord or lace. sne-°kór (sne 'khor) to warp, to get twisted Sch. - 2. sne-rgód (sne rgod), sne-dmár (sne dmar), sne-tsód (sne tshod), món-sne (mon sne), sneu (sneu), names of plants.
to shake, to cause to move slightly, bsném-byaisa-gżi (bsnem byaisa gzhi) a quagmire, shaking or yielding under one's feet Sch.; nem-ném bsném-pa (nem nem bsnem pa) Lex., pf. bsnems (bsnems).
(Cs. = snúr-ba (snur ba), to reduce to small pieces, to crumble.

snod (snod) 1. sbst. 1. vessel, snod-spyád (snod spyad) id., Lex. and col. frq.; gser-snód (gser snod) a gold vessel; pye-snód (phye snod) a vessel for meal or flour; ču-snód (chu snod) water-pot, pitcher; bu-snód (bu snod) uterus, womb, Lt. and col.; snód-kyi k'a (snod kyi kha) mouth of a vessel, snód-kyi żabs (snod kyi zhabs) bottom or foot of a vessel, stem of a glass. - 2. in anatomy: snod drug (snod drug) (the six vessels) are: gall-bladder, stomach, the small and the large intestine, urinary bladder and spermatic vessels (in the female: uterus); don-snód (don snod), the six vessels and the five don (don) together, v. don (don) 5. - 3. with reference to religion v. sde (sde), compounds. - 4. fig. 1. in ascetic language denoting man, as far as he is susceptible of higher and divine things; so already in Dzl. a man is called snod yóṅs-su dág-pa (snod yongs su dag pa) a very pure and holy vessel; snod-ldán slób-ma (snod ldan slob ma) a disciple eager to be instructed Mil.; snód-du rùṅ-ba (snod du rung ba) one fit for, worthy of (instruction); snód-du méd-pa (snod du med pa) unfit, insusceptible, rude, vulgar.; ṅés-par légs-pai snod mčog (nges par legs pai snod mchog), ṅes-legs bsgrúb-pai snod (nges legs bsgrub pai snod)
